coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of N,S co-doped carbon dots for fluorescence detection of Fe3+ ions and intracellular pH imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: CHEN, Xiao-Ping
ZHUANG, Ya-Feng
LIN, Jian-Wei
CHEN, Jing-Hua
HAN, Zhi-Zhong
coredata.dc:description: Using citric acid and thiourea as precursors, sulfur-nitrogen co-doped dual emission carbon dots (DECDs) were prepared by a simple solvothermal method. At the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, DECDs have two emission peaks located at 465 nm (blue) and 592 nm (yellow-green) which are attributed by core and surface of DECDs. It is found that Fe3+ ions can coordinate with oxygen-containing groups on DECDs, and then the electrons transfer from DECDs to Fe3+ ions, which quench the fluorescence of DECDs. The fluorescence intensity is linear with the concentration of Fe3+ ions in the range of 0–100 μM and the detection limit is 0.77 μM. Moreover, the designed sensor can be applied to detect Fe3+ ions in tap water with high selectivity, which provides a basis for the quantitative detection of Fe3+ ions. At the same time, DECDs can be used as a pH fluorescence probe. In the pH 2.5–11.5 range, the fluorescence increases with pH whilst the solution color changes, providing a visualization platform for pH detection. Moreover, the pH fluorescence probe can be used for intracellular pH fluorescence imaging in living cells, and DECDs have an excellent biocompatibility (e.g., cell viability remains about 80% after treatment for 24 h at 200 μg·mL−1 of DECDs). Therefore, DECDs has a certain application prospect in food detection, biological detection and determination of intracellular pH change.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fe3+ ions
pH detection
Fluorescence
Fluorescence imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: MTT assay for the cell cytotoxicity and cellular imaging
Optimization of synthesis conditions for DECDs
Determination of Fe3+ ions based on the DECDs
3.4 Mechanism of fluorescent sensing on Fe3+ ions
Determination of pH and intracellular pH imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fluorescence sensor for the detection of Fe3+ions and pH.
(a) The TEM and the (b) HRTEM diagram of DECDs.
(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum of DECDs. Insert: images of CDs under 365 nm and 465 nm lamps. (b) FTIR spectrum of DECDs.
(a) XPS survey and high-resolution surveys of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s, (e) S2p of DECDs.

(a) The influence of incubation time on the change of fluorescence. (b) Changes of fluorescence in the presence or absence of Fe3+ ions at different pH values. (c) The fluorescence spectra of DECDs with different concentrations of Fe3+ ions. (d) The relationship of the fluorescence intensity (I 550) versus Fe3+ ions concentration. (e) The effects of various ions on DECDs fluorescence.

(a) Fluorescence lifetime diagrams of DECDs and Fe3+/DECDs. (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of DECDs with different concentrations of Fe3+ ions. (c) FTIR of DECDs and Fe3+/DECDs. (d) Zeta potential of DECDs and Fe3+/DECDs.

(a) The linear relationship of the fluorescence intensity (I 550) versus pH values. (b) The UV–Vis absorption spectra of DECDs with different pH range from 2.0 to 12.0. Insert is a photo of DECDs with different pH under sunlight.
Comparison of Fe3+ ions detected at CDs from different sources or other nanomaterials.
Determination of Fe3+ ions in tap water with the designed sensor.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of N,S co-doped carbon dots for fluorescence detection of Fe3+ ions and intracellular pH imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350122, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Key Laboratory of Drug Target Discovery and Structural and Functional Research, Fuzhou 350122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Key Laboratory of Drug Target Discovery and Structural and Functional Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bFujian Key Laboratory of Drug Target Discovery and Structural and Functional Research, Fuzhou 350122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using citric acid and thiourea as precursors, sulfur-nitrogen co-doped dual emission carbon dots (DECDs) were prepared by a simple solvothermal method. At the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, DECDs have two emission peaks located at 465 nm (blue) and 592 nm (yellow-green) which are attributed by core and surface of DECDs. It is found that Fe3+ ions can coordinate with oxygen-containing groups on DECDs, and then the electrons transfer from DECDs to Fe3+ ions, which quench the fluorescence of DECDs. The fluorescence intensity is linear with the concentration of Fe3+ ions in the range of 0–100 μM and the detection limit is 0.77 μM. Moreover, the designed sensor can be applied to detect Fe3+ ions in tap water with high selectivity, which provides a basis for the quantitative detection of Fe3+ ions. At the same time, DECDs can be used as a pH fluorescence probe. In the pH 2.5–11.5 range, the fluorescence increases with pH whilst the solution color changes, providing a visualization platform for pH detection. Moreover, the pH fluorescence probe can be used for intracellular pH fluorescence imaging in living cells, and DECDs have an excellent biocompatibility (e.g., cell viability remains about 80% after treatment for 24 h at 200 μg·mL−1 of DECDs). Therefore, DECDs has a certain application prospect in food detection, biological detection and determination of intracellular pH change.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are small (< 10 nm) fluorescent nanomaterials and have been widely used in fluorescence [1], electrochemistry [2], and biological imaging [3] due to their excellent optical properties, low cost, good biocompatibility and low toxicity [4]. At present, most CDs developed by researchers are single emission, and there are few CDs with two emission wavelengths at one excitation wavelength. This kind of dual emission CDs can improve the effect of CDs in application. Studies have found that CDs are known to be composed of carbon nuclei and surface functional groups and the key factors affecting CDs dual emission mainly include particle spacing [5], reactive solvents [6], and precursors [7–9]. Therefore, we synthesize CDs by selecting solvents and using compounds containing N and S element.

Fe(III) ion is one of the important metal elements in human body, which plays an important role in cell metabolism and enzyme reaction [10] and also plays a crucial role in environmental pollution [11]. Therefore, the sensor used to detect Fe3+ ions is essential. At present, the methods used for the determination of Fe3+ ions include high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [12], colorimetry [13], atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [14], electrochemical analysis [15] and fluorescence analysis [16]. Fluorescence analysis has the advantages of simple operation, rapid operation and high sensitivity. The optical properties of CDs are stable and have been used as fluorescence probe for Fe3+ ions detection. For example, Won et al. synthesized coffee grounds derived CDs that could emit blue light by hydrothermal method for the detection of Fe3+ ions in aqueous solution [17]. Li et al. synthesized folic acid modified cerium doped CDs (CE-CDS-FA) by one-step hydrothermal method and one-pot method, in which Fe3+ ions could quench the fluorescence of the CE-CDS-FA, thus achieving the quantitative detection of Fe3+ ions [18].

Similarly, pH value in agriculture, chemistry, medicine and environmental systems and many other aspects play a crucial relationship [19]. pH value is one of the important parameters in water solution, pH value change will have a wide impact on drinking water, food and so on. In addition, long-term drinking of water with pH less than 6.5 will destroy the acid-base balance of human body, leading to the occurrence of various chronic diseases [20]. Therefore, the detection of pH value is of great significance to human health and environmental quality. However, many pH fluorescence probes reported so far monitor a narrow pH range [21,22]. For example, Wang et al. synthesized G-CDs by hydrothermal method to detect pH in cells with the range of 7.2–8.5 [23]. Chang et al. synthesized a dual-emission CDs whose fluorescence at 542 nm was sensitive to pH [24]. The detection ranges of the above pH sensors were relatively narrow. But in practical application, the narrow pH range limits the application of many probes, which requires a variety of detection methods. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a probe for pH fluorescence over a wide range. Intracellular pH fluctuations often occur in the cell cycle, apoptosis, and many other physiological [25]. Many common diseases are characterized by abnormal changes in cellular pH, such as the detection of intracellular pH can provide evidence for the early diagnosis of cancer [26]. Therefore, it is very important to develop a fluorescent probe for detecting intracellular pH changes.

Here, we propose a solvothermal method for the synthesis of N,S co-doped DECDs using citric acid and thiourea as precursors and formamide as solvent. In the presence of Fe3+ ions, carboxyl groups on DECDs chelate with Fe3+ ions, resulting in electron transfer, thus reducing the fluorescence intensity of DECDs and establishing a quantitative detection method for Fe3+ ions. Besides, with the increase of pH value, the fluorescence intensity of DECDs is increased due to protonation and deprotonation on its surface. In addition, DECDs can be used as a platform for pH visualization, and because of its low toxicity, it can also be used for fluorescence imaging of intracellular pH value (Scheme 1 ). The fluorescence sensor based on DECDs has broad application prospects in environmental monitoring, biological sensing and imaging fields.

Citric acid (C6H8O7, ≥99.5%), formamide (CH3NO, 99%), Iron (II) chloride (FeCl2), dimethyl sulfoxide (C2H6SO, DMSO) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Thiourea (CH4N2S, 99%), magnesium chloride (MgCl2·6H2O), barium chloride (BaCl2), calcium chloride dihydrate (CaCl2·2H2O) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O), potassium chloride (KCl), sodium chloride (NaCl) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, ≥98%) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich Trading Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco's modified eagie medium (DMEM) was purchased from ThermoFisher Biochemical Products Co., Ltd (Beijing, China).

The fluorescent spectra were recorded by a Lengguang F96Pro fluorescence spectrofluorometer (Shanghai, China). The ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a Thermo Fisher Scientific Gensys 150 spectrophotometer (Massachusetts, America). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was taken with a Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha+ X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Massachusetts, America). Fluorescence lifetime was collected by an Edinburgh FS5 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh, England). The Fourier infrared spectrum (FTIR) was recorded by a Thermo Fisher Scientific Nicolet IS50 fourier infrared spectrometer (Massachusetts, America). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were recorded by JEOL JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan). pH was detected by a pH S-3B precision acidity meter (Shanghai, China).

DECDs were synthesized by dissolving 0.6 g citric acid and 0.6 g thiourea in 10 mL of formamide and reacted at 180 °C for 8 h. Citric acid acts as a carbon source to provide carboxylic acid to bind formamide, while thiourea acts as an S dopant. Then the black liquid was centrifuged at 11,000 r min−1 for 15 min and filtered by microporous membrane (0.22 μm). The purified DECDs were preserved in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

Fluorescence measurement of Fe3+ ions was accomplished using the following steps. 80 μL 0.159 mg mL−1 dual emission carbon dots (DECDs) was added into a tube containing 2.90 mL ultrapure water, then added 20 μL Fe3+ ions solution of different concentrations. The fluorescence emission spectra of the samples were recorded at the excitation wavelengths of 550 nm.

To measure Fe3+ions in the real samples, tap water was used as natural sources. Tap water was collected from urban water piping. Then different concentrations of Fe3+ ions were added to the solution, including the real sample and DECDs probe. Finally, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at 550 nm excitation wavelengths.

The pH was measured at various pH in 0.2 mol L−1 disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4) - 0.1 mol L−1 citric acid buffer. Typically, 20 µL 1.1 mg mL−1 of DECDs solution was added to 3.0 mL of buffer with different pH values. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at the excitation wavelengths of 550 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MTT assay for the cell cytotoxicity and cellular imaging
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the biocompatibility of DECDs, the cytotoxicity of HeLa cells was studied in vitro by MTT assay. Hela cells in good biological condition were laid in 96-well plates at a cell density of 100 μL per well with 1 × 104 cells, and cultured for 24 h at 37 °C in 5% CO2. After discarding DMEM medium, 100 μL DECDs solution of different concentrations (0, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200 μg mL−1) was added, and incubated for 24 h, then the medium was sucked out, washed with PBS for 3 times, 100 μL MTT (1 mg mL−1) was added into each well, and then cultured for 4 h. After the medium was extracted, 150 μL DMSO was added to each well, and the cells were shaken on a shaking table for 10 min. Finally, the absorbance at 490 nm was measured by a microplate reader, and the cell survival rate was calculated.

Hela cells with good growth condition were laid in 6-well plates at a cell density of 1.0 × 105 cells per well and incubated at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 24 h. After removing the medium, DECDs solution was added and incubated for 1 h, and then it was sucked out and cleaned with PBS. Buffer solution with different pH was added and incubated for 30 min. Then the solution was sucked up, and finally observed under an inverted microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthesis conditions for DECDs
ce:section.ce:para: DECDs were prepared by fixing thiourea and citric acid as raw materials. Firstly, in order to prepare the DECDs, water and formamide were respectively selected as the reaction solvent. As can be seen from Fig. S1a, when water is used as the reaction solvent, the emission peak is only at 440 nm, while formamide is used as the solvent, the blue emission peak is red shifted slightly and a new emission peak appears. Secondly, different reaction times were set. The results can be seen from Fig. S1b that the fluorescence intensity of the two emission peaks is the maximum when the reaction time is 8 h, and the fluorescence intensity decreases when the reaction time is 10 h. Finally, we optimized the reaction temperatures, as shown in Fig. S1c. When the reaction temperatures are 180 °C and 200 °C, the fluorescence intensities have little difference. Therefore, we chose the lower temperature as the reaction temperature, and the final reaction condition was 180 °C and 8 h in formamide. As can be seen from Fig. S1d, a blue emission peak is formed when only formamide is used as raw materials, while two emission peaks are generated when thiourea, citric acid and formamide are used as raw materials. It may be because the dehydration condensation of the carboxyl group on citric acid and the amino group on formamide leads to C–N/C=N crosslinking, thus forming the graphite N structure, and formamide as a solvent could protect the sp2 conjugation and high graphite N content formed by DECDs in the carbon nucleus. Thiourea, as an S dopant, provided additional electrons for n-type doping, generated new excitation energy trap, reduced band gap energy, and led to the enhancement of fluorescence intensity [27].

The TEM image (Fig. 1 a) exhibits that CDs are spherical shape and monodisperse. The size distributes between 2 nm and 8 nm with average diameters of 5.04 nm. The HRTEM diagram of DECDs (Fig. 1b) shows the well-resolved lattice fringes spacing of 0.23 nm, corresponding to the (002) in-plane lattice fringe of carbon, verify the crystalline structure [28].

The UV-Vis absorption spectrum of DECDs shows four obvious absorption peaks at 293 nm, 337 nm, 520 nm and 564 nm (Fig. 2 a). The absorption peak at 240 nm is attributed to the π-π* electronic transition of C=C, the absorption peak at 337 nm is attributed to the n-π* electronic transition of C=O and the absorption peaks at 520 nm and 564 nm are attributed to the n-π* electronic transition of C–S and C=S [29]. The fluorescence spectrum of DECDs is shown in Fig. 2a. At the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, there are two fluorescence emission peaks at 465 nm and 592 nm. The yellow-green emission peak has the maximum fluorescence intensity at 589 nm under excitation of 550 nm. The inset in Fig. 2a shows an image of DECDs illuminated by 365 nm and 465 nm lamps, which reveals a strong blue and yellow fluorescence. As Fig. S2a shown, the blue emission peak (465 nm) moves with the change of excitation wavelength, while the yellow-green emission peak at 592 nm is slight shifted with different excitation wavelength. These indirectly explain the mechanism of dual emission that the two emissions are attributed to carbon core and surface group of DECDs, respectively. In order to further explore the fluorescence source of these two peaks, their fluorescence lifetime was tested. As shown in Fig. S2b, the fluorescence lifetimes of DECDs at 465 nm and 592 nm were 7.82 ns and 7.00 ns, respectively. The fluorescence attenuation at 592 nm was faster than that at 465 nm, indicating that the fluorescence emission at 592 nm was from the intrinsic state emission, while that at 465 nm was from the surface energy well emission.

The molecular structure of DECDs is identified by FTIR, and the result is shown in Fig. 2b. The characteristic peak at 1730 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C=O, the wide absorption peak at 3395 cm−1 belongs to O-H stretching vibration. The peaks at 3200 cm−1 are N-H stretching vibrations. The peak at 2828 cm−1 was the stretching vibration of –CH3. The characteristic peaks at 1357 cm−1 and 619 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of C=S and C–S respectively. The stretching vibration peak of 1100 cm−1 was C-N/C-O. In addition, the characteristic peak at 2064 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of N=C=S/N=C=O, indicating that the successful reaction of citric acid and thiourea and the successful doping of CDs with N and S elements. It also showed that the prepared DECDs had a large number of hydrophilic groups. And these results indicate that the prepared DECDs have lots of hydrophilic groups such as carboxyl (–COOH), amino (–NH2) and hydroxyl (–OH), so that DECDs can be well dispersed in the water.

Then the functional groups of DECDs are further identified by XPS. As shown in Fig. 3 a, there are four peaks at 162.99 eV, 285.17 eV, 399.83 eV and 531.44 eV, which belong to S2p, C1s, O1s and N1s, respectively. CDs are composed of C, N, S and O, indicating that the DECDs are successfully doped with N and S element. The high-resolution C1s spectrum exhibited five major peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.71 eV, 286.91 eV, 288.03 eV and 288.76 eV, respectively corresponding to C–C/C=C, C-N/C-O/C–S, N–C=O, COOH and C=S (Fig. 3b). The XPS spectrum of N1s (Fig. 3c) have three peaks at 398.28, 399.47 and 400.07 eV, which may be attributed to C-NH2, pyrrolic N and graphite N. The O1s XPS spectrum could be decomposed into two peaks, which corresponded to C=O (532.69 eV) and C-OH (531.27 eV) (Fig. 3d). In the S2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 3e), there are three peaks at 162.01, 163.29 and 167.96 eV, which are attributed to C–S–C (S2p 3/2), C–S–C (S2p 1/2) and C = S (S2p 1/2) [30]. The XPS results are consistent with the FTIR results, indicating that carboxyl and amino groups do exist on the CDs, and is successfully doped with N and S element. The doping of S element, graphite N and C-O are the factors affecting the red shift of CDs fluorescence emission peak. However, the synthesized DECDs had a emission peak of 592 nm due to the successful doping of S element and the large proportion of graphite N and C-O.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Fe3+ ions based on the DECDs
ce:section.ce:para: When two emission peaks were used for detection, the emission peak at 592 nm was relatively small and the changes were tiny. Moreover, most CDs used for fluorescence detection currently used blue emission peak, while fluorescence detection based on yellow-green emission peak was less. Therefore, 550 nm was used as the excitation wavelength in the future. Before DECDs was used to detect Fe3+ ions, the detection conditions were optimized. First of all, we optimized the reaction time, as shown in Fig. 4 a. Under the excitation of 550 nm, it keeps stable after 20 min. In addition, we investigated the influence of pH on the measurement of Fe3+ ions. As can be seen from Fig. 4b, Fe3+ ions has a great influence on the fluorescence intensity of DECDs when pH=5. However, under the condition of pH=5, as shown in Fig. S3a the detection range of Fe3+ ions linearity was narrow (1–10 μM), and the quenching value in pure water was larger than that at pH=5 (Fig. S3b). In addition, the buffer system may interfere with the accurate determination of Fe3+ ions at a high concentration, so quantitative detection of Fe3+ ions was finally selected under ultrapure water.

After added different concentrations of Fe3+ ions, the fluorescence intensity of DECDs decrease at 550 nm excitation with the increase of Fe3+ ions concentration (Fig. 4c). There is a good linear relationship between I 550 and Fe3+ ions concentration in the range of 0–100 μM (Fig. 4d). The linear relationship is I 550 = −9.43C Fe 3+ + 1239.45 (R 2 = 0.994) and a detection limit (LOD) of 0.77 μM is determined at S/N = 3, which meets the European Union (EC) requirement that the maximum permissible concentration of Fe3+ions in drinking water is 3.57 μM [32–37]. Compared with CDs from other sources and other nanomaterials used in the detection of Fe3+ ions (Table 1 ), this work has certain advantages, with a wide detection range and a relatively low detection limit than some sensors, which was lower than international standards.

In order to study the selectivity of the sensor for Fe3+ ions, some common inorganic ions, such as K +, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Ba2+, NH4 +, Cr3+, Zn2+, Hg+, Pb2+, Al3+ and Fe2+ ions, were added into the DECDs solution, the concentration of these ions was 100 μM and fluorescence spectra were detected under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 4e, the fluorescence intensity of DECDs changes little with the addition of different metal ions except Fe3+ ions, which could be ignored. The results show that DECDs fluorescent probe can selectively recognize Fe3+ ions.

In order to further verify the feasibility of this sensor, it is applied to detect Fe3+ in tap water. The results are shown in Table 2 . Then Fe3+ ions with different concentrations (1 μM, 8 μM, 80 μM) is added into tap water, the recovery rate of fluorescence method is between 96.7% and 105.43% in tap water. Therefore, the constructed fluorescent sensor could be used to detect Fe3+ ions in tap water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 3.4 Mechanism of fluorescent sensing on Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to understand the mechanism of the quenching of DECDs by Fe3+ ions, firstly, we investigated whether the quenching mechanism of CDs by Fe3+ ions was dynamic or static. We tested the fluorescence lifetime of DECDs and Fe3+/DECDs. As can be seen from Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence lifetime between them does not change much, so the quenching mechanism is static quenching. Moreover, Stern-Volmer equation was used to calculate the quenching constant:
(1)
I 0
/ I =
K
sv
· c + 1 =
K
q
·
τ 0
· c + 1
where I 0 and I are the 550 nm fluorescence intensity of DECDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions, K sv and K q are Stern-Volmer quenching constant and quenching rate constant, τ 0 is the fluorescence lifetime, and c is the concentration of Fe3+ ions. As shown in Fig. S4, the Stern-Volmer plot drawn between the ratio of fluorescence intensities (I 0/I) and Fe3+ ion concentration shows a good linearity with the estimated K sv of 7.50 × 103 M−1. In this paper, τ 0 is about 7.0 ns, so K q is calculated as about 1.07 × 1012 M−1 s−1, which is far higher than the largest K q of collisional quenching of 2 × 1010 M−1 s−1 [38]. It demonstrates that the quenching of DECDs by Fe3+ ions is static.

Furthermore, we studied the changes of UV-Vis absorption spectra after adding different concentrations of Fe3+ ions. As shown in Fig. 5b, the UV-Vis absorption peak at 540 nm red-shifts with the increase of Fe3+ ion concentration. This change can be attributed to the formation of Fe3+-NH or Fe3+-OH complexes by Fe3+and functional groups on the surface of DECDs. To further investigate the quenching mechanism, we determined the Zeta potential and FTIR spectra of DECDs to determine that Fe3+ ions are coordinated with oxygen or amino groups on DECDs. It can be seen from the FTIR spectra (Fig. 5c) of DECDs and Fe3+/DECDs, after the addition of Fe3+ ions, the C=O peak at 1677 cm−1 disappears, and the –OH peak around 3300 cm−1 becomes smaller. These results indicate that Fe3+ oxygen-containing groups at the DECDs. In addition, as shown in Fig. 5d, the Zeta potential of DECDs changes from −30 mV to −10 mV after adding Fe3+ ions, indicating that Fe3+ ions coordinate with oxygen-containing groups on DECDs and the chelation efficiency of Fe3+ ions with oxygen-containing group. Therefore, the electrons transfer from CDs to Fe3+ ions and cause the fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of pH and intracellular pH imaging
ce:section.ce:para: After synthesizing DECDs, we studied the change of DECDs fluorescence value at different pH values. We find that the fluorescence intensity (I 550) of DECDs increased with the increase of pH value. As shown in Fig. 6 a, I 550 has a good linear relationship with pH value between pH = 2.5–11.5, the linear relationship is I 550 = 111.3 pH – 218.68 (R 2 = 0.984). In addition, under sunlight, we find that the color of the solution changes with varied pH (Fig. 6b). According to the UV–Vis absorption spectra of different pH values, the absorption peak at 560 nm was blue shifted with the increase of pH value, and the change of n-π* electron transition energy indicated that the pH value of the solvent would change the surface state energy level of DECDs, thus affecting its fluorescence properties. The above results demonstrated that the fluorescence emission peaks of DECDs under 550 nm were sensitive to pH, which could be used as a novel pH visual sensing fluorescence probe. In addition, the Zeta potential of DECDs solutions with different pH values is characterized, and the result obtained from Fig. S5 displays that the Zeta potential decreases with the increase of pH. These demonstrate that the protonation and aprotonation happen on the surface of DECDs.

The toxicity of DECDs to Hela cells was studied by MTT assay. As shown in Fig. S6a, the survival rates of Hela cells incubated under DECDs of different concentrations for 24 h are all above 80%, displaying that DECDs have low cytotoxicity and could be used for cell imaging. In order to further study the cell imaging ability of DECDs under different pH, HeLa cells were cultured with various pH. The results as shown in Fig. S6b exhibit that the fluorescence in HeLa cells with higher pH value is stronger, while the fluorescence with lower pH value is weaker, which is consistent with the linear relationship of pH. The results show that DECDs can be used for intracellular pH monitoring.

In this work, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped dual emission carbon dots were prepared by solvothermal method. The two emission peaks may correspond to the carbon core of DECDs and the surface groups which may be protonated or deprotonated under different pH conditions. Therefore, the fluorescence based on DECDs could be changed with the change of pH, and the color of DECDs solution could be seen to change with different pH under sunlight. DECDs could be used as a probe to detect fluctuations in intracellular pH in Hela cells. In addition, the fluorescence based on DECDs can be quenched by Fe3+ ions, which may be due to the chelation coordination between DECDs surface groups and Fe3+ ions. In summary, the synthesized DECDs could be used for the determination of Fe3+ ions in the environment and pH detection in various biological fields. It has a wide application prospect in the fields of biosensors and biomedical imaging.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (51602053), Joint Funds for the Innovation of Science and Technology, Fujian Province (2017Y9122), Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2019J01300), Program for New Century Excellent Talents in Fujian Province University (2018B031).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.cjac.2022.100151.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Environmentally benign conversion of waste polyethylene terephthalate to fluorescent carbon dots for “on-off-on” sensing of ferric and pyrophosphate ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Hu, Z. Gao, J. Yang, H. Chen, L. Han, Environmentally benign conversion of waste polyethylene terephthalate to fluorescent carbon dots for “on-off-on” sensing of ferric and pyrophosphate ions, J Colloid Interface Sci538 (2019) 481–8.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical sensor based on N-doped carbon dots decorated with manganese oxide nanospheres for simultaneous detection of p-aminophenol and paracetamol

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Feng, Y. Li, S. Yu, Q. Yang, Y. Tong, B.C. Ye, Electrochemical sensor based on N-doped carbon dots decorated with manganese oxide nanospheres for simultaneous detection of p-aminophenol and paracetamol, Analyst146 (2021) 5135–42.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Yeast cryptococcus podzolicus derived fluorescent carbon dots for multicolour cellular imaging and high selectivity detection of pollutant

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Ji, X. Yuan, H. Nian, P. Song, Y. Xiang, Y. Wei, S. Wang, K. Qin, Q. Zhang, Y. Tu, Yeast cryptococcus podzolicus derived fluorescent carbon dots for multicolour cellular imaging and high selectivity detection of pollutant, Dyes Pigm. 182 (2020) 108621.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive ratiometric fluorescence probe based on chitosan carbon dots and calcein for Alkaline phosphatase detection and bioimaging in cancer cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Mu, X. Jiang, Y. Zhang, X. Liu, S. Zhang, W. Wang, Y. Huang, P. Ma, D. Song, Sensitive ratiometric fluorescence probe based on chitosan carbon dots and calcein for Alkaline phosphatase detection and bioimaging in cancer cells, Anal Chim Acta. 1188 (2021) 339163.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Interparticle distance as a key factor for controlling the dual-emission properties of carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.J. Yoo, B.E. Kwak, D.H. Kim, Interparticle distance as a key factor for controlling the dual-emission properties of carbon dots, Phys Chem Chem Phys22 (2020) 20227–37.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of dual-emissive carbon dots with a unique solvatochromism phenomenon

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Wu, W. Li, Y. Sun, X. Zhang, J. Zhuang, H. Hu, B. Lei, C. Hu, Y. Liu, Synthesis of dual-emissive carbon dots with a unique solvatochromism phenomenon, J Colloid Interface Sci555 (2019) 607–14.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Toward efficient dual-emissive carbon dots through sulfur and nitrogen co-doped

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Zhou, J. Zhuang, W. Li, C. Hu, B. Lei, Y. Liu, Toward efficient dual-emissive carbon dots through sulfur and nitrogen co-doped, J Mater Chem C5 (2017) 8014–21.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of dual-emission fluorescent carbon quantum dots and their ratiometric fluorescence detection for arginine in 100% water solution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, H. Liu, H. Song, Yu M., Wei L., Li Z.Synthesis of dual-emission fluorescent carbon quantum dots and their ratiometric fluorescence detection for arginine in 100% water solution. New J Chem43 (2019) 13234–9.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-emission carbon dots for ratiometric detection of Fe3+ ions and acid phosphatase

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Pang, S. Liu, Dual-emission carbon dots for ratiometric detection of Fe3+ ions and acid phosphatase, Anal Chim Acta1105 (2020) 155–61.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for highly selective on-off detection of Fe3+ ions in real samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Hashemi, M.H. Mousazadeh, Preparation of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for highly selective on-off detection of Fe3+ ions in real samples, Opt Mater121 (2021) 111515.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile and green synthesis of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots from betel leaves for sensitive detection of picric acid and iron ion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Kalanidhi, P. Nagaraaj, Facile and green synthesis of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots from betel leaves for sensitive detection of picric acid and iron ion. J Photochem Photobiol, A418 (2021) 113369.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reversed-phase HPLC determination of Co(II), Ni(II) and Fe(III) as their 2-(2-thiazolylazo)-5-dimethylaminophenol chelates

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.Y. Li, M.D. Gui, Y.Q. Zhao, Reversed-phase HPLC determination of Co(II), Ni(II) and Fe(III) as their 2-(2-thiazolylazo)-5-dimethylaminophenol chelates, Talanta42 (1995) 89–92.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Small-scale ultrasound-assisted extraction of phenolics from pomegranate peels and their application as a natural reagent for the colorimetric assay of Iron

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Siriangkhawut, P. Didpinrum, Y. Khanhuathon, K. Ponhong, K. Grudpan, Small-scale ultrasound-assisted extraction of phenolics from pomegranate peels and their application as a natural reagent for the colorimetric assay of Iron, Anal Lett53 (2020) 887–904.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Xie, W. Liu, X. Hao, X. Jiang, Y. Zhong, T. Liu, M. Wang, Cloud point extraction combined with flame atomic absorption spectrometry for the determination of copper, iron and zinc using 2-(5-bromo-2-pyridylazo)-5-diethylaminophenol- tetraphenylborate ion-pairing agent, Chem Pap75 (2021) 5849–59.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, J. Ma, Y. Zhang, N. Xu, X. Wang, A series of cobalt-based coordination polymer crystalline materials as highly sensitive electrochemical sensors for detecting trace Cr(VI), Fe(III) ions, and ascorbic acid, Cryst Growth Des21 (2021) 4390–7.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Shin, F. Jannah, E.J. Yoo, J. Kim, A colorimetric and fluorescence “turn-on” sensor for Fe(III) ion based on imidazole-functionalized polydiacetylene, Sens Actuat B Chem350 (2022) 130885.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Won, J. Kim, The detection of Fe (III) and ascorbic acid by fluorescence quenching and recovery of carbon dots prepared from coffee waste, Korean J Chem Eng (2022).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Li, Z. Guo, T. Liu, F. Yu, J. Zeng, Y. Zhang, L. Yin, X. Liu, H. Jiang, X. Wang, Folic acid-modified cerium-doped carbon dots as photoluminescence sensors for cancer cells identification and Fe(III) detection, Chemosensors10 (6) (2022), 219.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, H. Wang, Y. Niu, Y. Wang, L. Feng, A facile AIE fluorescent probe for broad range of pH detection, Spectrochim Acta A226 (2020) 117650.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Lu, D. Chen, Y. Duan, W. Zhang, Y. Xie, Y. Chen, X. Wang, New properties of carbon nanomaterials through zinc doping and application as a ratiometric fluorescence pH sensor, Mater Res Bull142 (2021) 111410.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Lei, Y. Fu, Y. Wu, L. Chen, J. Liang, A ratiometric fluorescent probe for pH detection based on Ag2S quantum dots-carbon dots nanohybrids, R Soc Open Sci7 (2020) 200482.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Wang, L. Shi, J. Zhao, S. Shuang, Fluorescent carbon dots with real-time nucleolus-monitoring capability for gene delivery and biosensing of NO2– and pH, Appl Surf Sci599 (2022) 153902.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Chang, Z. Zhao, H. Shi, J. Feng, Y. Yang, L. Shi, Ratiometric fluorescent carbon dots for enantioselective sensing of L-lysine and pH discrimination in vivo and in vitro, Sens Actuators B362 (2022) 131792.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.Z. Yang, N. Xiao, S.G. Liu, L. Han, N.B. Li, H.Q. Luo, pH-induced aggregation of hydrophilic carbon dots for fluorescence detection of acidic amino acid and intracellular pH imaging, Mater Sci Eng C Mater108 (2020) 110401.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Na, Y. Han, Y. Chen, S. Ma, J. Liu, X. Chen, Synthesis of silicon nanoparticles emitting yellow-green fluorescence for visualization of pH change and determination of intracellular pH of living cells, Anal Chem93 (2021) 5185–93.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Xu, Y. Wang, L. Sun, Q. Qi, X. Zhao, Chitosan and κ-carrageenan-derived nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots "on-off-on" fluorescent probe for sequential detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid, Int J Biol Macromol191 (2021) 1221–7.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Li, L. Shi, J. Jia, O. Eltayeb, W. Lu, Y. Tang, C. Dong, S. Shuang, Red fluorescent carbon dots for tetracycline antibiotics and pH discrimination from aggregation-induced emission mechanism, Sens Actuat B Chem. 332 (2021) 129513.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Deng, J. Qian, Y. Zhou, F. Lu, Regulatory preparation of N/S doped carbon quantum dots and their applications as Fe(III) ion sensors, ChemistrySelect. 5 (2020) 5306–11.
